content.text: This is exactly the kind of reframing that changes the analysis — let me engage each point, and do the research point 5 asks for. First let me load web tools and read the podman VM prototype you pointed me at, in parallel.

content.content:
1	= Podman VM Supply Chain
2	
3	== Motivation
4	
5	Podman is an excellent open-source tool, but its VM image distribution model creates a serious reproducibility hazard. After months of stable development using a particular Podman version, deleting and redownloading the VM with the same semantic version silently produced a fundamentally different image. The entire SENTRY/BOTTLE network architecture — which had been working reliably — broke. Virtual networking inside containers stopped functioning, and the failure mode gave no indication that the underlying VM image had changed.
6	
7	Systematic investigation (documented in Study/study-net-namespace-permutes/) revealed that the new VM image carried Podman 5.3+ security hardening that prohibited `setns()` on externally-created network namespaces. This broke Recipe Bottle's core isolation mechanism: containers could no longer join pre-configured network namespaces, regardless of privileges or creation method. The definitive post-mortem in Study/study-net-namespace-permutes/claudeopus4-netns-fail-memo.md confirmed this was not a configuration issue but a fundamental change in the VM's capabilities. The earlier networking journey is recorded in podman-gateway-proposal.md and the iptables architecture failures in Study/study-shared-pod-userid/ and Study/study-shared-pod-ip-forward/.
8	
9	The root cause is quay.io's build and retention policy. The upstream registry rebuilds VM images approximately every three hours and deletes older versions. Version tags are not immutable within a Podman semantic version — downloading the same tag at different times produces different disk images with different capabilities. There is no way to recover a previous image once it has been replaced.
10	
11	This motivated bringing VM images under user control by mirroring them into the project's own Depot artifact registry. By maintaining stable, versioned VM images in a controlled repository, Recipe Bottle can ensure reproducible environments and prevent silent regressions from upstream image churn.
12	
13	== Constraints
14	
15	Several technical and architectural constraints shape the VM supply chain design.
16	
17	VM images are OCI artifacts rather than standard container images. They contain compressed root filesystem tarballs for WSL environments or CoreOS disk images for standard virtualization platforms. These artifacts use non-standard media types, rely on custom annotations such as disktype to indicate their intended use, and carry opaque blobs rather than composable filesystem layers. While they are platform-indexed in the OCI sense, they are not platform-runnable as containers would be. Because of these differences, standard `podman pull` and `docker pull` cannot correctly retrieve and reassemble VM artifacts — they operate at the container-runtime abstraction level, which assumes runnable images with standard layer structure. Tools like crane (manifest and blob-level registry operations), skopeo (cross-registry copying with format awareness), and oras (OCI Registry As Storage, designed for non-container artifacts) work below that abstraction at the registry API and manifest level, which is why they can handle VM artifacts correctly.
18	
19	The bootstrap process faces a chicken-and-egg problem. The mirroring workflow requires crane, oras, or skopeo, but Recipe Bottle does not want to obligate users to install and maintain these tools on their host systems. The solution is to use an ignite VM, initialized with the latest uncontrolled upstream image, as a disposable tooling environment. Crane and jq are installed inside the ignite VM, and all registry operations execute there. The ignite VM is discarded after completing the mirroring operation.
20	
21	Establishing provenance for VM images is difficult. When podman machine init runs, it prints a SHA digest during initialization that can be captured and compared against expected values. However, this information is only available at initialization time. After a VM has been created, there is no reliable way to verify which specific image was used to initialize it. This limitation makes post-hoc auditing challenging and emphasizes the importance of capturing provenance information during the init operation.
22	
23	The technical constraint that VM images are locked to a specific Podman semantic version but not immutable within that version creates ongoing maintenance challenges. Two distinct image families exist: machine-os-wsl for WSL environments and machine-os for standard CoreOS-based systems. These families have different build processes, different artifact structures, and different platform variants, requiring parallel handling in the mirroring workflow.
24	
25	Storage cost considerations favor a conservative approach. Users do not frequently update their Podman VM installations, so maintaining a few full disk images in the Depot is practical. The storage expense is acceptable given the stability and reproducibility benefits.
26	
27	== Architecture
28	
29	The VM supply chain architecture centers on a two-machine pattern. The ignite machine serves as a temporary, disposable tooling environment. It is initialized with whatever upstream image Podman naturally fetches and is used only to run crane, podman, and other OCI utilities needed for manifest introspection and blob manipulation. The deploy machine, by contrast, is the persistent, user-controlled VM initialized from carefully selected and cached images stored in the Depot.
30	
31	The overall lifecycle follows a sequence of operations: check, mirror, fetch, init, and start or stop. The check operation discovers upstream updates by comparing digests between the current registry state and what is available upstream. The mirror operation queries upstream manifests for both WSL and standard image families, downloads individual disk blobs, repackages them as container images, and pushes them to the registry with platform-specific tags. The fetch operation pulls the platform-specific container image from the registry and extracts the disk image to a local cache directory. The init operation initializes the deploy VM from the cached disk image and writes a brand file containing full provenance information. Finally, start and stop operations manage the deploy VM's runtime state. A nuke operation destroys all VMs, both ignite and deploy, along with the local cache, enabling a complete reset of the environment.
32	
33	This separation between ignite and deploy machines provides a clear boundary between uncontrolled tooling infrastructure and the production-quality VM environment that hosts containers.
34	
35	== Operations
36	
37	=== Check (unimplemented)
38	
39	Discovers when new VM images are available upstream by comparing digest values between the current registry state and what quay.io currently offers. This operation was specified in earlier documentation but has never been implemented in bash.
40	
41	=== Mirror
42	
43	The most complex operation. Steps:
44	
45	. Bootstrap the ignite VM with crane and jq installed
46	. Query upstream manifests for both machine-os-wsl and machine-os image families at the specified Podman version
47	. Parse manifests to identify all available platform variants (architecture + disk type)
48	. For each platform variant, download the individual disk image blob from upstream using `crane blob`
49	. Repackage each blob as a container image: a simple Dockerfile copies the disk blob into a scratch-based container, with an identity file for traceability
50	. Build each platform-specific image locally within the ignite VM
51	. Push to the registry with tags encoding the Podman version, a timestamp-based identity, and the platform specification
52	
53	The result is a set of individually addressable container images, each containing a specific disk image for a specific platform variant.
54	
55	=== Fetch
56	
57	Pulls a platform-specific container image from the registry to the local workstation:
58	
59	. Start the ignite VM to access the podman image cache
60	. Authenticate to the registry
61	. Pull the platform-specific container image matching the user's station configuration
62	. Extract the disk image tarball from the container
63	. Write it to the local cache directory (always overwrites existing)
64	
65	The cached file serves as the source for subsequent VM initialization.
66	
67	=== Init
68	
69	Creates the deploy VM from the cached disk image:
70	
71	. Validate that the requested platform matches one of the available manifest platforms
72	. Run `podman machine init` with the rootful flag and the path to the cached image
73	. Start the VM temporarily to write a brand file containing the full provenance chain: Podman version, VM image origin, fully qualified image name, digest, and identity timestamp
74	. Stop the VM, leaving it ready for normal use
75	
76	The brand file provides an auditable record of exactly which image was used to create the VM.
77	
78	=== Start / Stop
79	
80	Straightforward lifecycle commands. Start brings the deploy VM online for container operations. Stop gracefully shuts it down. These do not affect the ignite VM.
81	
82	=== Nuke
83	
84	Complete environment reset:
85	
86	. Stop and remove all containers
87	. Destroy both the ignite and deploy VMs
88	. Delete the VM cache directory
89	
90	Requires explicit confirmation. Used when switching Podman versions, recovering from corrupted VM states, or performing clean test runs.
91	
92	== Configuration
93	
94	The VM supply chain relies on two categories of configuration variables. Regime configuration variables are stored in the repository and apply to all users working within the Recipe Bottle project. Station configuration variables are stored on each workstation and reflect local machine characteristics.
95	
96	The regime configuration includes several key variables. RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME specifies the name for the temporary tooling VM. RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME specifies the name for the persistent production VM. RBRM_CRANE_TAR_GZ provides the URL for downloading the crane binary into the ignite VM. RBRM_CHOSEN_PODMAN_VERSION identifies which Podman semantic version to mirror and use. RBRM_CHOSEN_VMIMAGE_ORIGIN records the upstream source registry. RBRM_CHOSEN_IDENTITY is a timestamp-based identifier assigned during the mirror operation to distinguish different builds of the same Podman version. RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS lists the platform variants that must be available in the upstream manifest and should be mirrored to the registry. RBRR_GITHUB_PAT_ENV provides the path to the credentials file used for registry authentication during mirror and fetch operations.
97	
98	The station configuration includes variables that vary by workstation. RBRS_PODMAN_ROOT_DIR points to the base directory where Podman stores machine data. RBRS_VMIMAGE_CACHE_DIR specifies where fetched disk images should be cached locally. RBRS_VM_PLATFORM identifies which platform variant the local machine should use, such as mow_amd64_wsl or mos_arm64_base.
99	
100	These variables are defined in the RBRM (Machine) regime at `rbmm_moorings/rbrm.env`, separated from the broader RBRR configuration to clarify the distinction between Recipe Bottle's application-level configuration and the underlying VM infrastructure configuration.
101	
102	An important transitional note concerns GHCR-era variables. The original implementation targeted GitHub Container Registry and used variables like RBRR_REGISTRY_OWNER, RBRR_REGISTRY_NAME, and RBRR_GITHUB_PAT_ENV to manage authentication and registry addressing. These variables are being deleted as part of ongoing work. When Google Artifact Registry support is implemented, new properly scoped variables will be minted following the project's prefix naming discipline.
103	
104	== Implementation Status
105	
106	The current implementation exists in Tools/rbk/rbv_PodmanVM.sh and targets GitHub Container Registry. This bash module implements the mirror, fetch, init, start, stop, and nuke operations as described above. However, the check operation, which would discover upstream updates by comparing digests, has no bash implementation and exists only as a concept.
107	
108	The codebase reflects an evolution well beyond the original specifications. Earlier specification documents RBSVC and RBSVM described a simpler design in which crane would copy entire images directly from upstream to the registry. This design envisioned variables like rbrr_chosen_vmimage_fqin and rbrr_chosen_vmimage_sha that would reference complete images. These variables were never implemented in code and remain spec-only ghosts.
109	
110	The actual bash implementation evolved to a more sophisticated approach involving blob-level extraction, repackaging of disk images as container images, support for both WSL and standard image families, iteration across multiple platform variants, and identity stamping for build traceability. This evolution reflects lessons learned during implementation about the structure of OCI VM artifacts and the need for fine-grained control over individual disk images.
111	
112	The vme.extractor.sh script was an earlier standalone tool for manifest introspection. It has been superseded by the zrbv_process_image_type function in the modern bash module, which integrates manifest parsing directly into the mirroring workflow.
113	
114	The file rbp.podman.mk contains historical Makefile prototypes from the GHCR era. These Makefile targets served as an initial exploration of the VM mirroring workflow but have been fully superseded by the rbv_PodmanVM.sh module, which provides a more robust and maintainable implementation.
115	
116	The implementation has never been ported from GitHub Container Registry to Google Artifact Registry. This migration remains necessary for production use within the Recipe Bottle architecture, which standardizes on Google Cloud infrastructure for its depot and service account management.
117

file.content:
= Podman VM Supply Chain

== Motivation

Podman is an excellent open-source tool, but its VM image distribution model creates a serious reproducibility hazard. After months of stable development using a particular Podman version, deleting and redownloading the VM with the same semantic version silently produced a fundamentally different image. The entire SENTRY/BOTTLE network architecture — which had been working reliably — broke. Virtual networking inside containers stopped functioning, and the failure mode gave no indication that the underlying VM image had changed.

Systematic investigation (documented in Study/study-net-namespace-permutes/) revealed that the new VM image carried Podman 5.3+ security hardening that prohibited `setns()` on externally-created network namespaces. This broke Recipe Bottle's core isolation mechanism: containers could no longer join pre-configured network namespaces, regardless of privileges or creation method. The definitive post-mortem in Study/study-net-namespace-permutes/claudeopus4-netns-fail-memo.md confirmed this was not a configuration issue but a fundamental change in the VM's capabilities. The earlier networking journey is recorded in podman-gateway-proposal.md and the iptables architecture failures in Study/study-shared-pod-userid/ and Study/study-shared-pod-ip-forward/.

The root cause is quay.io's build and retention policy. The upstream registry rebuilds VM images approximately every three hours and deletes older versions. Version tags are not immutable within a Podman semantic version — downloading the same tag at different times produces different disk images with different capabilities. There is no way to recover a previous image once it has been replaced.

This motivated bringing VM images under user control by mirroring them into the project's own Depot artifact registry. By maintaining stable, versioned VM images in a controlled repository, Recipe Bottle can ensure reproducible environments and prevent silent regressions from upstream image churn.

== Constraints

Several technical and architectural constraints shape the VM supply chain design.

VM images are OCI artifacts rather than standard container images. They contain compressed root filesystem tarballs for WSL environments or CoreOS disk images for standard virtualization platforms. These artifacts use non-standard media types, rely on custom annotations such as disktype to indicate their intended use, and carry opaque blobs rather than composable filesystem layers. While they are platform-indexed in the OCI sense, they are not platform-runnable as containers would be. Because of these differences, standard `podman pull` and `docker pull` cannot correctly retrieve and reassemble VM artifacts — they operate at the container-runtime abstraction level, which assumes runnable images with standard layer structure. Tools like crane (manifest and blob-level registry operations), skopeo (cross-registry copying with format awareness), and oras (OCI Registry As Storage, designed for non-container artifacts) work below that abstraction at the registry API and manifest level, which is why they can handle VM artifacts correctly.

The bootstrap process faces a chicken-and-egg problem. The mirroring workflow requires crane, oras, or skopeo, but Recipe Bottle does not want to obligate users to install and maintain these tools on their host systems. The solution is to use an ignite VM, initialized with the latest uncontrolled upstream image, as a disposable tooling environment. Crane and jq are installed inside the ignite VM, and all registry operations execute there. The ignite VM is discarded after completing the mirroring operation.

Establishing provenance for VM images is difficult. When podman machine init runs, it prints a SHA digest during initialization that can be captured and compared against expected values. However, this information is only available at initialization time. After a VM has been created, there is no reliable way to verify which specific image was used to initialize it. This limitation makes post-hoc auditing challenging and emphasizes the importance of capturing provenance information during the init operation.

The technical constraint that VM images are locked to a specific Podman semantic version but not immutable within that version creates ongoing maintenance challenges. Two distinct image families exist: machine-os-wsl for WSL environments and machine-os for standard CoreOS-based systems. These families have different build processes, different artifact structures, and different platform variants, requiring parallel handling in the mirroring workflow.

Storage cost considerations favor a conservative approach. Users do not frequently update their Podman VM installations, so maintaining a few full disk images in the Depot is practical. The storage expense is acceptable given the stability and reproducibility benefits.

== Architecture

The VM supply chain architecture centers on a two-machine pattern. The ignite machine serves as a temporary, disposable tooling environment. It is initialized with whatever upstream image Podman naturally fetches and is used only to run crane, podman, and other OCI utilities needed for manifest introspection and blob manipulation. The deploy machine, by contrast, is the persistent, user-controlled VM initialized from carefully selected and cached images stored in the Depot.

The overall lifecycle follows a sequence of operations: check, mirror, fetch, init, and start or stop. The check operation discovers upstream updates by comparing digests between the current registry state and what is available upstream. The mirror operation queries upstream manifests for both WSL and standard image families, downloads individual disk blobs, repackages them as container images, and pushes them to the registry with platform-specific tags. The fetch operation pulls the platform-specific container image from the registry and extracts the disk image to a local cache directory. The init operation initializes the deploy VM from the cached disk image and writes a brand file containing full provenance information. Finally, start and stop operations manage the deploy VM's runtime state. A nuke operation destroys all VMs, both ignite and deploy, along with the local cache, enabling a complete reset of the environment.

This separation between ignite and deploy machines provides a clear boundary between uncontrolled tooling infrastructure and the production-quality VM environment that hosts containers.

== Operations

=== Check (unimplemented)

Discovers when new VM images are available upstream by comparing digest values between the current registry state and what quay.io currently offers. This operation was specified in earlier documentation but has never been implemented in bash.

=== Mirror

The most complex operation. Steps:

. Bootstrap the ignite VM with crane and jq installed
. Query upstream manifests for both machine-os-wsl and machine-os image families at the specified Podman version
. Parse manifests to identify all available platform variants (architecture + disk type)
. For each platform variant, download the individual disk image blob from upstream using `crane blob`
. Repackage each blob as a container image: a simple Dockerfile copies the disk blob into a scratch-based container, with an identity file for traceability
. Build each platform-specific image locally within the ignite VM
. Push to the registry with tags encoding the Podman version, a timestamp-based identity, and the platform specification

The result is a set of individually addressable container images, each containing a specific disk image for a specific platform variant.

=== Fetch

Pulls a platform-specific container image from the registry to the local workstation:

. Start the ignite VM to access the podman image cache
. Authenticate to the registry
. Pull the platform-specific container image matching the user's station configuration
. Extract the disk image tarball from the container
. Write it to the local cache directory (always overwrites existing)

The cached file serves as the source for subsequent VM initialization.

=== Init

Creates the deploy VM from the cached disk image:

. Validate that the requested platform matches one of the available manifest platforms
. Run `podman machine init` with the rootful flag and the path to the cached image
. Start the VM temporarily to write a brand file containing the full provenance chain: Podman version, VM image origin, fully qualified image name, digest, and identity timestamp
. Stop the VM, leaving it ready for normal use

The brand file provides an auditable record of exactly which image was used to create the VM.

=== Start / Stop

Straightforward lifecycle commands. Start brings the deploy VM online for container operations. Stop gracefully shuts it down. These do not affect the ignite VM.

=== Nuke

Complete environment reset:

. Stop and remove all containers
. Destroy both the ignite and deploy VMs
. Delete the VM cache directory

Requires explicit confirmation. Used when switching Podman versions, recovering from corrupted VM states, or performing clean test runs.

== Configuration

The VM supply chain relies on two categories of configuration variables. Regime configuration variables are stored in the repository and apply to all users working within the Recipe Bottle project. Station configuration variables are stored on each workstation and reflect local machine characteristics.

The regime configuration includes several key variables. RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME specifies the name for the temporary tooling VM. RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME specifies the name for the persistent production VM. RBRM_CRANE_TAR_GZ provides the URL for downloading the crane binary into the ignite VM. RBRM_CHOSEN_PODMAN_VERSION identifies which Podman semantic version to mirror and use. RBRM_CHOSEN_VMIMAGE_ORIGIN records the upstream source registry. RBRM_CHOSEN_IDENTITY is a timestamp-based identifier assigned during the mirror operation to distinguish different builds of the same Podman version. RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS lists the platform variants that must be available in the upstream manifest and should be mirrored to the registry. RBRR_GITHUB_PAT_ENV provides the path to the credentials file used for registry authentication during mirror and fetch operations.

The station configuration includes variables that vary by workstation. RBRS_PODMAN_ROOT_DIR points to the base directory where Podman stores machine data. RBRS_VMIMAGE_CACHE_DIR specifies where fetched disk images should be cached locally. RBRS_VM_PLATFORM identifies which platform variant the local machine should use, such as mow_amd64_wsl or mos_arm64_base.

These variables are defined in the RBRM (Machine) regime at `rbmm_moorings/rbrm.env`, separated from the broader RBRR configuration to clarify the distinction between Recipe Bottle's application-level configuration and the underlying VM infrastructure configuration.

An important transitional note concerns GHCR-era variables. The original implementation targeted GitHub Container Registry and used variables like RBRR_REGISTRY_OWNER, RBRR_REGISTRY_NAME, and RBRR_GITHUB_PAT_ENV to manage authentication and registry addressing. These variables are being deleted as part of ongoing work. When Google Artifact Registry support is implemented, new properly scoped variables will be minted following the project's prefix naming discipline.

== Implementation Status

The current implementation exists in Tools/rbk/rbv_PodmanVM.sh and targets GitHub Container Registry. This bash module implements the mirror, fetch, init, start, stop, and nuke operations as described above. However, the check operation, which would discover upstream updates by comparing digests, has no bash implementation and exists only as a concept.

The codebase reflects an evolution well beyond the original specifications. Earlier specification documents RBSVC and RBSVM described a simpler design in which crane would copy entire images directly from upstream to the registry. This design envisioned variables like rbrr_chosen_vmimage_fqin and rbrr_chosen_vmimage_sha that would reference complete images. These variables were never implemented in code and remain spec-only ghosts.

The actual bash implementation evolved to a more sophisticated approach involving blob-level extraction, repackaging of disk images as container images, support for both WSL and standard image families, iteration across multiple platform variants, and identity stamping for build traceability. This evolution reflects lessons learned during implementation about the structure of OCI VM artifacts and the need for fine-grained control over individual disk images.

The vme.extractor.sh script was an earlier standalone tool for manifest introspection. It has been superseded by the zrbv_process_image_type function in the modern bash module, which integrates manifest parsing directly into the mirroring workflow.

The file rbp.podman.mk contains historical Makefile prototypes from the GHCR era. These Makefile targets served as an initial exploration of the VM mirroring workflow but have been fully superseded by the rbv_PodmanVM.sh module, which provides a more robust and maintainable implementation.

The implementation has never been ported from GitHub Container Registry to Google Artifact Registry. This migration remains necessary for production use within the Recipe Bottle architecture, which standardizes on Google Cloud infrastructure for its depot and service account management.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Ubuntu WSL rootfs tarball downloads from cloud-images.ubuntu.com:

## Available Ubuntu WSL Rootfs Tarballs

Ubuntu 22.04 (Jammy) is available at https://cloud-images.ubuntu.com/wsl/jammy/current/ubuntu-jammy-wsl-amd64-wsl.rootfs.tar.gz, and Ubuntu Mantic is available at https://cloud-images.ubuntu.com/wsl/mantic/current/ubuntu-mantic-wsl-amd64-wsl.rootfs.tar.gz.

For Ubuntu 24.04 (Noble), the available files include ubuntu-noble-wsl-amd64-wsl.rootfs.tar.gz and ubuntu-noble-wsl-arm64-wsl.rootfs.tar.gz, which are described as "File system image and Kernel packed".

## How to Use the Tarballs

After downloading, use wsl --import to install the tarball. The command format is: wsl --import <Distribution Name> <Installation Folder> <Ubuntu WSL2 Image Tarball path>.

The official Ubuntu Cloud Images website at cloud-images.ubuntu.com hosts these WSL-specific rootfs tarballs organized by release version and architecture (amd64, arm64).

Based on the search results, here's what I found about WSL distributions.json manifest and tar download URLs:

## Official Microsoft WSL Distribution Registry

You can find URLs to download distribution files at DistributionInfo.json for your chosen distro. The official distribution manifest is located at https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/blob/master/distributions/DistributionInfo.json, which contains download URLs and SHA-256 checksums for various Linux distributions available through WSL.

## Distribution Manifest Details

To add a distribution to the --install list, you submit a pull request on the WSL GitHub repository (https://github.com/microsoft/WSL) that updates the DistributionInfo.json file with your distribution's details.

WSL distributions are defined by a tar file with a .wsl file extension on Windows, and the TAR file contains the root file system of a Linux distribution (all the distribution files), as well as the WSL configuration files.

## Available Distributions

The DistributionInfo.json file includes official distributions such as:
- Ubuntu (multiple versions)
- Debian
- Kali Linux
- openSUSE
- SUSE Linux Enterprise
- AlmaLinux

Each distribution entry in the manifest includes download URLs for both x86_64 and ARM64 architectures, along with SHA-256 checksums for verification.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about importing WSL distributions using the `wsl --import` command without the Microsoft Store:

## Basic Import Command

Use the command `wsl --import <Distro> <InstallLocation> <FileName>` to import the tar file. You can use any Linux distribution inside of the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL), even if it is not available in the Microsoft Store, by importing it with a tar file.

## Obtaining the Tar File

First you'll need to obtain a tar file that contains all the Linux binaries for the distribution. You can obtain one by:
- Downloading a provided tar file (for example, Alpine Linux offers Mini Root Filesystem downloads)
- Finding a Linux distribution container and exporting an instance as a tar file

## File Format Support

The command works with tar.gz files, not just .tar files. In fact, Alpine distribution available in Microsoft Store does exactly that.

## Practical Example

Deploying a distribution can be done with the `wsl --import <distro> <deployment_folder> <image>` command. For example: `wsl --import ubuntu-20.04-dotnetcore E:\WSL\ubuntu-20.04-dotnetcore E:\Downloads\ubuntu-20.04-server-cloudimg-amd64-wsl.rootfs.tar.gz`

This approach allows you to set up Linux distributions in WSL2 without going through the Microsoft Store.

content.text:
Yes — and the research settles the linchpin question decisively in your favor. Let me give you the finding first, because it cascades through everything else.

## The research answer (points 1 & 5): yes, the cloud can slurp WSL with no Windows in the loop

A WSL distro is **just a gzipped rootfs tarball** — the `.wsl` format is literally a tar of the root filesystem plus config, and `wsl --import` consumes a `.tar.gz` directly. The bytes are published over plain HTTPS by two independent authorities:

- **Canonical** publishes the Ubuntu WSL rootfs at `cloud-images.ubuntu.com/wsl/<release>/current/ubuntu-<release>-wsl-amd64-wsl.rootfs.tar.gz` (and arm64), organized by release and arch.
- **Microsoft** publishes `DistributionInfo.json` — a public manifest listing, per distro, the **download URL + SHA-256 checksum** for x86_64 and ARM64.

So a Cloud Build job can: `curl` the rootfs straight from Canonical → verify against a published SHA-256 → wrap as an OCI artifact (the same opaque-blob-as-scratch-image trick `rbv_PodmanVM.sh` already uses) → push to GAR. **The workstation never touches the canonical bytes.** That is exactly your "trust GCP over the potentially-corrupted workstation" posture — and it's strictly *cleaner* than the paddock's `wsl --export`, which trusted whatever the operator happened to have installed.

Two bonuses fall out:

1. **You can bypass Microsoft entirely for the bytes.** Pull from Canonical, and use Canonical's own `SHA256SUMS` (published next to the rootfs) as the checksum authority rather than Microsoft's `DistributionInfo.json`. Given the anti-lock-in theme, pin the vendor you least distrust as the digest authority — for Ubuntu that's Canonical, not Microsoft.
2. **It kills the podman-VM "ignite VM" machination too.** Re-read RBSPV §Architecture: the disposable-ignite-VM hack exists *only* because they were mirroring on a workstation that lacked `crane`/`skopeo`/`oras`. On Cloud Build those tools are natively available — no chicken-and-egg, no throwaway podman machine. Moving podvm acquisition cloud-side doesn't just unify it with `wsl`; it *simplifies* it below what the prototype does today.

**Consequence: my Hole 1 dissolves.** Once acquisition is cloud-side for *all* kinds, there is no split execution model. Everything runs on an egress-permitted Cloud Build pool (never the airgap pool — acquisition inherently needs egress, same as today's enshrine). The kind enum goes back to gating *parameters and fetch mechanism*, not *where the code runs and who signs* — which is the RBRV-analogy-sized fork the paddock claimed, and now it's true.

## Point 2: this is the reframing that matters, and it makes me revise

You've named a different value proposition than the one I critiqued. My "switch statement wearing a recipe noun's clothes" jab assumed the payoff was supposed to be **implementation reuse**. If the payoff is **user-facing cognitive-load reduction** — fewer cult verbs/nouns a workstation operator must carry — then internal per-kind dispatch isn't a smell. It's a **facade**, and a facade is the correct tool for cognitive load. "As simple as possible, but no simpler" resolves cleanly: the *interface* is the simple part that unifies (one verb, one recipe noun, one mental model); the *fetch mechanism* is the irreducible part that legitimately stays per-kind and hidden.

So I withdraw that critique — but it's replaced by a sharper test, not nothing:

> **A facade only reduces cognitive load if it doesn't leak.** The discipline is to ruthlessly push per-kind difference *below* the verb surface. Wherever a kind forces kind-specific knowledge back into the operator's face — different required params, different consumption ritual, different failure vocabulary — that's where the abstraction is "no simpler than possible," and that's the budget you're actually managing. A facade that leaks gives you a new layer *on top of* the same special cases: strictly worse than no facade.

And your provenance instinct is right — let me give it a formulation that, I think, is *more* defensible than the paddock's current framing. The paddock justifies the recipe noun by "everything is a GAR `package`." That's a *storage* invariant — true but shallow. The real load-bearing invariant is the **acquisition-provenance envelope**:

> Every Lode `{INSTANCE_NOUN}`, regardless of kind, carries: **source coordinate · upstream digest/checksum · acquired-at · acquiring identity (the Cloud Build SA) · vouch signature.** Composite kinds nest it (a cohort = a set of sub-acquisitions + an assembly attestation over them).

*That* is what justifies one recipe noun across heterogeneous bytes — not the storage shape, the **semantic** shape. It's the captured-side parallel to the built-side SLSA, and naming it this way makes the Vessel:Lode analogy load-bearing where it's actually true.

One honest nuance to bake in: the provenance *type* differs. A built Hallmark's vouch attests **build** provenance (SLSA L3, "made securely by this builder"). An acquired `{INSTANCE_NOUN}`'s vouch attests **capture** provenance ("we faithfully fetched upstream X at digest D at time T"). Both are real, both belong in the envelope, and they are *not the same claim*. Your "as appropriate for whatever the blob is" is exactly the right calibration — just make the spec say out loud that acquired-side vouch ≠ built-side vouch, so a future session doesn't try to force SLSA-build semantics onto a mirrored tarball.

## Point 4: what to bake into the paddock now (you're right not to defer these)

Three are *architecture*, not pace-detail, and your predecessor wrongly left them to the heat→pace process:

1. **Acquisition is cloud-side for every kind.** Strike the `wsl --export` framing; replace with HTTPS-fetch-from-vendor + checksum-verify on Cloud Build. Note the bonus retirement of the podvm ignite-VM hack. This is the change that makes the whole unification structurally honest.

2. **Scope Lode to the producer/acquisition side only — explicitly.** This is the resolution to my Hole 3, and it's a *scoping* fix, not hand-waving. The recipe noun unifies *acquisition + provenance envelope*; it does **not** unify *consumption*. Consumption stays per-kind by design: Cloud Build FROM-line (`base`), tool-mount (`tool`/`reliquary`), host `wsl --import` (`wsl`), host podman (`podvm-*`). And — this matters — **narrow the "resist the asymmetry" premise** (paddock line 9). As written it tells a future session to suppress the instinct that consumption is asymmetric. That instinct is *correct*. Rewrite it to: "the analogy is load-bearing for acquisition operations; it deliberately does **not** extend to consumption, which is irreducibly per-kind." Otherwise that premise will lure someone into inventing a `yoke`-for-hosts that shouldn't exist.

3. **Name the payload shapes.** It's not "one OCI format + compound manifests." It's three: native layered image (`base`/`tool`), opaque-blob-wrapped-as-OCI (`wsl`/`podvm-*` — the rootfs tar / disk blob), and compound-manifest-of-refs (`reliquary`/podvm compounds). The provenance envelope rides uniformly on all three; the wire format does not. Saying this prevents a future "why doesn't the wsl Lode look like a base Lode" thrash.

And the **one hole that none of this dissolves** — keep it red:

> **Compound delete + shared-blob reference semantics.** GAR dedups layers; a date-cohort reliquary and a podvm compound share blobs with their siblings/sub-Lodes. The captured-side delete verb needs a refcount / orphan-sweep / immutable-cohort decision *designed now*, because it's the one operation that silently destroys data (the "interior-hole regression" the paddock already fears). This is genuine architecture and must not wait for a pace to stumble into it.

## Revised verdict

I've moved. With (1) acquisition cloud-side — which research confirms is not just feasible but the *better* design — (2) the unifying invariant named as the provenance envelope and the verb understood as a facade for cognitive load, and (3) Lode explicitly producer-side-only, **the full six-kind unification is defensible**, and the cognitive-load argument is a legitimate "as simple as possible." I'm no longer recommending you sever the heat. The clean reasons to do this are real, and they're *your* reasons (vocabulary load, provenance-on-everything), not the implementation-reuse reasons I initially imputed.

What remains is design work, not a go/no-go: the compound-delete semantics (hole 4), the `{INSTANCE_NOUN}` + delete-verb naming with the capture-vs-build attestation nuance respected, and the non-vessel consumer landing (which is now cleanly *out of Lode's scope* — it's a consumption-side question for caparison-w / podman station config).

Next step, your call: I can **draft the paddock amendments** (the three bake-now items + narrowed premise + the red compound-delete flag), or first **read `rbv_PodmanVM.sh` and the caparison-w specs** to nail the compound-delete and consumption realities against actual code before we commit words to the paddock. I'd lean toward reading the bash first — compound-delete is the one place I'm reasoning from a doc, not the code.

**Sources:**
- [Microsoft Learn — Import any Linux distribution to use with WSL](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/use-custom-distro)
- [Microsoft Learn — Build a Custom Linux Distro for WSL](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/build-custom-distro)
- [microsoft/WSL — distributions/DistributionInfo.json](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/blob/master/distributions/DistributionInfo.json)
- [Canonical cloud-images — WSL rootfs (24.04)](https://cloud-images.ubuntu.com/wsl/releases/24.04/current/)
- [Canonical cloud-images — WSL rootfs (jammy)](https://cloud-images.ubuntu.com/wsl/jammy/current/)
- [DEV — Manually installing WSL2 distributions (import from tar.gz)](https://dev.to/milolav/manually-installing-wsl2-distributions-41b4)

1	#!/bin/bash
2	# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
3	#
4	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
5	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
6	# You may obtain a copy of the License at
7	#
8	#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
9	#
10	# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
11	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
12	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
13	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
14	# limitations under the License.
15	#
16	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
17	#
18	# Recipe Bottle VM - Podman Virtual Machine Management
19	
20	######################################################################
21	# WARNING: This implementation targets GHCR (GitHub Container Registry).
22	# It has NOT been ported to Google Artifact Registry (GAR/Depot).
23	# The operations are functionally correct but the registry target is
24	# obsolete for this project.
25	# See lenses/RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc for context.
26	######################################################################
27	
28	set -euo pipefail
29	
30	# Multiple inclusion detection
31	test -z "${ZRBV_INCLUDED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbv multiply included - check sourcing hierarchy"
32	ZRBV_INCLUDED=1
33	
34	######################################################################
35	# Internal Functions (zrbv_*)
36	
37	zrbv_kindle() {
38	  # Build module variables from furnished environment (already validated by CLI)
39	
40	  # Module Variables (ZRBV_*)
41	  readonly ZRBV_GIT_REGISTRY="ghcr.io"
42	
43	  readonly ZRBV_IGNITE_INIT_STDOUT="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/ignite_init_stdout.txt"
44	  readonly ZRBV_IGNITE_INIT_STDERR="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/ignite_init_stderr.txt"
45	  readonly ZRBV_DEPLOY_INIT_STDOUT="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/deploy_init_stdout.txt"
46	  readonly ZRBV_DEPLOY_INIT_STDERR="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/deploy_init_stderr.txt"
47	
48	  readonly ZRBV_FACT_DIGEST_PREFIX="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/fact-digest-"
49	  readonly ZRBV_FACT_SOURCE_PREFIX="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/fact-source-"
50	  readonly ZRBV_FACT_EXTENSION_PREFIX="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/fact-extension-"
51	  readonly ZRBV_FACT_ARCH_PREFIX="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/fact-arch-"
52	  readonly ZRBV_FACT_DISKTYPE_PREFIX="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/fact-disktype-"
53	
54	  readonly ZRBV_EMPLACED_BRAND_FILE="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/emplaced_brand_file.txt"
55	  readonly ZRBV_GENERATED_BRAND_FILE="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/generated_brand_file.txt"
56	  readonly ZRBV_NATURAL_TAG_FILE="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/natural_tag.txt"
57	
58	  readonly ZRBV_PODMAN_REMOVE_PREFIX="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/podman_inspect_remove_"
59	  readonly ZRBV_IDENTITY_TS_FILE="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/identity_timestamp.txt"
60	  readonly ZRBV_MOW_MANIFEST_JSON="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/mow_manifest.json"
61	  readonly ZRBV_MOS_MANIFEST_JSON="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/mos_manifest.json"
62	  readonly ZRBV_MOW_ENTRIES_JSON="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/mow_entries.json"
63	  readonly ZRBV_MOS_ENTRIES_JSON="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/mos_entries.json"
64	  readonly ZRBV_MOW_DECODED_PREFIX="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/mow_decoded_"
65	  readonly ZRBV_MOS_DECODED_PREFIX="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/mos_decoded_"
66	
67	  readonly ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR="/tmp/rbv-upload"
68	  readonly ZRBV_VM_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR}/manifest_"
69	  readonly ZRBV_VM_BLOB_PREFIX="${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR}/blob_"
70	
71	  readonly ZRBV_BLOB_INFO="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/blob_info.txt"
72	  readonly ZRBV_LAYERS_JSON="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/layers.json"
73	
74	  readonly ZRBV_VMIMAGE_TAG_PREFIX="${ZRBV_GIT_REGISTRY}/${RBRR_REGISTRY_OWNER}/${RBRR_REGISTRY_NAME}:podvm-${RBRM_CHOSEN_PODMAN_VERSION}-"
75	
76	  # Additional temp file paths
77	  readonly ZRBV_SCRIPT_DIR="$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")"
78	
79	  readonly ZRBV_KINDLED=1
80	}
81	
82	zrbv_sentinel() {
83	  # Guard that kindle ran before function execution
84	  test "${ZRBV_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbv not kindled - call zrbv_kindle first"
85	}
86	
87	# Generate brand file content
88	zrbv_generate_brand_file() {
89	  test ! -f "${ZRBV_GENERATED_BRAND_FILE}" || buc_die 'Brand file already exists'
90	
91	  {
92	    echo "# Recipe Bottle VM Brand File"
93	    echo "#"
94	    echo "PODMAN_VERSION: ${RBRM_CHOSEN_PODMAN_VERSION}"
95	    echo "VMIMAGE_ORIGIN: ${RBRM_CHOSEN_VMIMAGE_ORIGIN}"
96	    echo "VMIMAGE_FQIN:   ${RBRM_CHOSEN_VMIMAGE_FQIN}"
97	    echo "VMIMAGE_DIGEST: ${RBRM_CHOSEN_VMIMAGE_DIGEST}"
98	    echo "IDENTITY:       ${RBRM_CHOSEN_IDENTITY}"
99	  } > "${ZRBV_GENERATED_BRAND_FILE}"
100	}
101	
102	# Extract natural tag from podman init output
103	zrbv_extract_natural_tag() {
104	  local z_init_output_file="$1"
105	
106	  local z_init_line
107	  local z_tag
108	  while IFS= read -r z_init_line; do
109	    case "${z_init_line}" in *"Looking up Podman Machine image at"*)
110	      z_tag="${z_init_line#*Looking up Podman Machine image at }"
111	      z_tag="${z_tag% to create VM*}"
112	      printf '%s\n' "${z_tag}"
113	      break
114	    ;; esac
115	  done < "${z_init_output_file}" > "${ZRBV_NATURAL_TAG_FILE}"
116	
117	  test -s "${ZRBV_NATURAL_TAG_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to extract natural tag from init output"
118	}
119	
120	# Compare two files and return error code if different
121	zrbv_is_different_predicate() {
122	  local z_file1="$1"
123	  local z_file2="$2"
124	
125	  local z_content1="$(<"${z_file1}")"
126	  local z_content2="$(<"${z_file2}")"
127	
128	  if test "${z_content1}" = "${z_content2}"; then
129	    return 0
130	  else
131	    buc_warn "File content mismatch detected!"
132	    buc_warn "File 1 (${z_file1}) contents:"
133	    echo "${z_content1}"
134	    buc_warn "File 2 (${z_file2}) contents:"
135	    echo "${z_content2}"
136	    return 1
137	  fi
138	}
139	
140	# Stop and remove a VM if it exists
141	zrbv_remove_vm() {
142	  local z_vm_name="$1"
143	
144	  if podman machine inspect "${z_vm_name}" > "${ZRBV_PODMAN_REMOVE_PREFIX}${z_vm_name}.txt"; then
145	    buc_info       "Stopping ${z_vm_name}..."
146	    podman machine stop     "${z_vm_name}" || buc_warn "Failed to stop ${z_vm_name} during _remove_vm"
147	    buc_info       "Removing ${z_vm_name}..."
148	    podman machine rm -f    "${z_vm_name}" || buc_die "Failed to remove ${z_vm_name}"
149	  else
150	    buc_info             "VM ${z_vm_name} does not exist. Nothing to remove."
151	  fi
152	}
153	
154	# Prepare ignite machine: start if present, or fully reinit if force_reinit=true
155	zrbv_ignite_bootstrap() {
156	  local z_force_reinit="${1}"
157	
158	  buc_info "Bootstrapping ignite machine: ${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}"
159	
160	  if podman machine inspect "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" &>/dev/null; then
161	    buc_info "Stopping existing ignite machine (if running)..."
162	    podman machine stop "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" 2>/dev/null \
163	      || buc_die "Could not stop existing ignite VM cleanly"
164	  fi
165	
166	  if test "${z_force_reinit}" = "true"; then
167	    buc_step "Removing existing ignite machine due to force_reinit..."
168	    zrbv_remove_vm "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" || buc_die "Removal failed."
169	  fi
170	
171	  if ! podman machine inspect "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" &>/dev/null; then
172	    buc_step "Creating ignite VM with natural podman init..."
173	    podman machine init --log-level=debug      "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" \
174	                                            2> "${ZRBV_IGNITE_INIT_STDERR}"  \
175	         | "${ZRBV_SCRIPT_DIR}/rbupmis_Scrub.sh" "${ZRBV_IGNITE_INIT_STDOUT}"  \
176	      || buc_die "Bad init."
177	
178	    buc_step "Starting ignite machine..."
179	    podman machine start "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" || buc_die "Failed to start ignite machine"
180	
181	    buc_step "Installing crane..."
182	    podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --                        \
183	        "curl -sL ${RBRM_CRANE_TAR_GZ} | sudo tar -xz -C /usr/local/bin crane" \
184	        || buc_die "crane fail"
185	
186	    buc_step "Verify crane installation..."
187	    podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" -- \
188	        "crane version" || buc_die "crane confirm fail."
189	
190	    buc_step "Installing tools..."
191	    podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" -- \
192	        "sudo dnf install -y jq" || buc_die "tools install fail"
193	  else
194	    buc_step "Restarting ignite machine..."
195	    podman machine start "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" || buc_die "Failed to restart ignite machine"
196	  fi
197	
198	  buc_step "Ignite machine ready with crane installed"
199	}
200	
201	# Helper function to process one image type
202	zrbv_process_image_type() {
203	  local z_manifest_file="$1"
204	  local z_entries_file="$2"
205	  local z_decoded_prefix="$3"
206	  local z_prefix="$4"
207	  local z_fqin="$5"
208	  local z_family_name="$6"
209	
210	  buc_step "Retrieving manifest for ${z_family_name} image: ${z_fqin}"
211	  podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --        \
212	        "crane manifest ${z_fqin} | jq ." > "${z_manifest_file}" \
213	      || buc_die "Failed to retrieve ${z_family_name} manifest"
214	
215	  buc_step "${z_family_name} Manifest:"
216	  cat "${z_manifest_file}"
217	
218	  buc_step "Extract manifest entries for ${z_family_name}..."
219	  jq -r '.manifests[] | @base64' "${z_manifest_file}" > "${z_entries_file}" \
220	    || buc_die "Failed to extract ${z_family_name} entries"
221	
222	  buc_step "${z_family_name} family images:"
223	  local z_entry_num=0
224	  while IFS= read -r z_entry; do
225	    z_entry_num=$((z_entry_num + 1))
226	    local       z_decoded="${z_decoded_prefix}${z_entry_num}.json"
227	    local     z_arch_file="${z_decoded_prefix}${z_entry_num}_arch.txt"
228	    local z_disktype_file="${z_decoded_prefix}${z_entry_num}_disktype.txt"
229	    local   z_digest_file="${z_decoded_prefix}${z_entry_num}_digest.txt"
230	
231	    openssl enc -base64 -d <<< "${z_entry}"  > "${z_decoded}"
232	    jq -r '.platform.architecture'          "${z_decoded}" > "${z_arch_file}"
233	    jq -r '.annotations.disktype // "base"' "${z_decoded}" > "${z_disktype_file}"
234	    jq -r '.digest'                         "${z_decoded}" > "${z_digest_file}"
235	
236	    local z_arch z_disktype z_digest
237	    z_arch=$(<"${z_arch_file}")
238	    z_disktype=$(<"${z_disktype_file}")
239	    z_digest=$(<"${z_digest_file}")
240	    local z_platform_spec="${z_prefix}_${z_arch}_${z_disktype}"
241	
242	    echo "${z_digest}"      > "${ZRBV_FACT_DIGEST_PREFIX}${z_platform_spec}"
243	    echo "${z_fqin}"        > "${ZRBV_FACT_SOURCE_PREFIX}${z_platform_spec}"
244	    echo "${z_arch}"        > "${ZRBV_FACT_ARCH_PREFIX}${z_platform_spec}"
245	    echo "${z_disktype}"    > "${ZRBV_FACT_DISKTYPE_PREFIX}${z_platform_spec}"
246	
247	    echo "  ${ZRBV_GIT_REGISTRY}/${RBRR_REGISTRY_OWNER}/${RBRR_REGISTRY_NAME}:podvm-${RBRM_CHOSEN_IDENTITY}-${RBRM_CHOSEN_PODMAN_VERSION}-${z_platform_spec}"
248	  done < "${z_entries_file}"
249	}
250	
251	######################################################################
252	# External Functions (rbv_*)
253	
254	rbv_nuke() {
255	  # Name parameters
256	  local z_yes_opt="${1:-}"
257	
258	  # Handle documentation mode
259	  buc_doc_brief "Completely reset the podman virtual machine environment"
260	  buc_doc_oparm "yes_opt" "If 'YES', then skip confirmation step"
261	  buc_doc_lines "Destroys ALL containers, VMs, and VM cache"
262	  buc_doc_lines "Requires explicit YES confirmation"
263	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
264	
265	  # Ensure module started
266	  zrbv_sentinel
267	
268	  # Perform command
269	  buv_dir_exists "${RBRS_PODMAN_ROOT_DIR}" || buc_warn "Podman directory not found."
270	
271	  buc_step "WARNING: This will destroy all podman VMs and cache found in ${RBRS_PODMAN_ROOT_DIR}"
272	
273	  # Skip confirmation if YES was passed as parameter
274	  test "${z_yes_opt}" = "YES" || buc_require "This will destroy all podman VMs and cache!" "YES"
275	
276	  buc_step "Stopping all containers..."
277	  podman stop -a  || buc_warn "Attempt to stop all containers failed; normal if machine not started."
278	
279	  buc_step "Removing all containers..."
280	  podman rm -a -f || buc_warn "Attempt to remove all containers failed; normal if machine not started."
281	
282	  buc_step "Removing all podman machines..."
283	  for z_vm in $(podman machine list -q); do
284	    z_vm="${z_vm%\*}"  # Remove trailing asterisk indicating 'current vm'
285	    zrbv_remove_vm "${z_vm}" || buc_die "Attempt to remove VM ${z_vm} failed."
286	  done
287	
288	  buc_step "Deleting VM cache directory..."
289	  rm -rf "${RBRS_PODMAN_ROOT_DIR}/machine"/* || buc_die "Failed to clear VM machine cache"
290	
291	  buc_success "Podman VM environment reset complete"
292	}
293	
294	# Mirror VM images to GHCR
295	rbv_mirror() {
296	  # Handle documentation mode
297	  buc_doc_brief "Process manifests for WSL and standard machine-os images, then upload needed disk images to GHCR"
298	  buc_doc_lines "Queries quay.io/podman/machine-os-wsl and quay.io/podman/machine-os"
299	  buc_doc_lines "Uses crane manifest to retrieve raw manifests, formatted with jq"
300	  buc_doc_lines "Validates that all RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS are available"
301	  buc_doc_lines "Downloads needed disk images and packages them as container images"
302	  buc_doc_lines "Builds all images locally, creates local manifest list, then single push to GHCR"
303	  buc_doc_lines "Tags each platform image individually for direct access"
304	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
305	
306	  # Ensure module started
307	  zrbv_sentinel
308	
309	  # Perform command
310	  buc_step "Prepare fresh ignite machine with crane and tools..."
311	  zrbv_ignite_bootstrap false || buc_die "Failed to create temp machine"
312	
313	  buc_step "Generate new identity for this build..."
314	  date +'%Y%m%d-%H%M%S' > "${ZRBV_IDENTITY_TS_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to generate identity timestamp"
315	  local z_new_identity
316	  z_new_identity=$(<"${ZRBV_IDENTITY_TS_FILE}")
317	  test -n "${z_new_identity}" || buc_die "Empty identity timestamp from ${ZRBV_IDENTITY_TS_FILE}"
318	  buc_info "New identity: ${z_new_identity}"
319	
320	  buc_step "Potential container image names for caching:"
321	
322	  # Process each image type
323	  zrbv_process_image_type        \
324	    "${ZRBV_MOW_MANIFEST_JSON}"  \
325	    "${ZRBV_MOW_ENTRIES_JSON}"   \
326	    "${ZRBV_MOW_DECODED_PREFIX}" \
327	    "mow"                        \
328	    "quay.io/podman/machine-os-wsl:${RBRM_CHOSEN_PODMAN_VERSION}" \
329	    "Machine-OS-WSL"
330	
331	  zrbv_process_image_type        \
332	    "${ZRBV_MOS_MANIFEST_JSON}"  \
333	    "${ZRBV_MOS_ENTRIES_JSON}"   \
334	    "${ZRBV_MOS_DECODED_PREFIX}" \
335	    "mos"                        \
336	    "quay.io/podman/machine-os:${RBRM_CHOSEN_PODMAN_VERSION}" \
337	    "Machine-OS standard"
338	
339	  buc_step "Validating RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS availability..."
340	
341	  local z_missing_images=""
342	  for z_needed_image in ${RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS}; do
343	    if test ! -f "${ZRBV_FACT_DIGEST_PREFIX}${z_needed_image}"; then
344	      z_missing_images="${z_missing_images} ${z_needed_image}"
345	    else
346	      buc_info "Found needed image: ${z_needed_image}"
347	    fi
348	  done
349	
350	  test -z "${z_missing_images}" || buc_die "Missing required disk images:${z_missing_images}"
351	
352	  buc_step "All needed disk images are available in upstream manifests"
353	
354	  buc_step "Setting up VM temporary dir..."
355	  podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --              \
356	      "rm -rf ${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR} && mkdir -p ${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR}" \
357	    || buc_die "Failed to create VM temp directory"
358	
359	  buc_step "Building container images..."
360	  for z_needed_image in ${RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS}; do
361	    buc_step "Processing: ${z_needed_image}"
362	
363	    # Read facts
364	    local z_digest z_source_fqin
365	    z_digest=$(<"${ZRBV_FACT_DIGEST_PREFIX}${z_needed_image}")
366	    z_source_fqin=$(<"${ZRBV_FACT_SOURCE_PREFIX}${z_needed_image}")
367	
368	    podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --                                       \
369	        "crane manifest ${z_source_fqin}@${z_digest} | jq -r '.layers[]'" > "${ZRBV_LAYERS_JSON}" \
370	      || buc_die "Failed to extract layers for ${z_needed_image}"
371	
372	    jq -r 'select(.annotations."org.opencontainers.image.title" // .mediaType | test("disk|raw|tar|qcow2|machine")) | .digest + ":" + .mediaType' \
373	           "${ZRBV_LAYERS_JSON}" > "${ZRBV_BLOB_INFO}" \
374	      || buc_die "Failed to find disk blob for ${z_needed_image}"
375	
376	    local z_blob_info
377	    IFS= read -r z_blob_info < "${ZRBV_BLOB_INFO}" || true
378	
379	    test -n "${z_blob_info}" || buc_die "No disk blob found in manifest for ${z_needed_image}"
380	
381	    local z_blob_digest="${z_blob_info%:*}"
382	    local z_temp="${z_blob_info#*:}"
383	    local z_media_type="${z_temp#*:}"
384	
385	    local z_extension="tar"
386	    case "${z_media_type}" in
387	      *zstd*) z_extension="tar.zst" ;;
388	      *gzip*) z_extension="tar.gz"  ;;
389	      *xz*)   z_extension="tar.xz"  ;;
390	    esac
391	
392	    echo "${z_extension}" > "${ZRBV_FACT_EXTENSION_PREFIX}${z_needed_image}"
393	
394	    local z_vm_blob_file="${ZRBV_VM_BLOB_PREFIX}${z_needed_image}.${z_extension}"
395	    podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --              \
396	        "crane blob ${z_source_fqin}@${z_blob_digest} > ${z_vm_blob_file}" \
397	      || buc_die "Failed to download blob for ${z_needed_image}"
398	
399	    local z_dockerfile_name="Dockerfile.${z_needed_image}"
400	    local z_dockerfile_path="${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR}/${z_dockerfile_name}"
401	
402	    podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --             \
403	        "echo '${z_new_identity}' > ${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR}/identity.txt" \
404	      || buc_die "Failed to create identity file"
405	
406	    {
407	        echo "FROM scratch"
408	        echo "COPY blob_${z_needed_image}.${z_extension} /disk-image.tar"
409	        echo "COPY identity.txt /identity.txt"
410	    } | podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" -- \
411	        "cat > ${z_dockerfile_path}"                          \
412	      || buc_die "Failed to create Dockerfile for ${z_needed_image}"
413	
414	    local z_local_tag="localhost/podvm-${z_needed_image}:${z_new_identity}"
415	
416	    podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --                                  \
417	        "cd ${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR} && podman build -f ${z_dockerfile_name} -t ${z_local_tag} ." \
418	      || buc_die "Failed to build image for ${z_needed_image}"
419	
420	  done
421	
422	  buc_step "All container images built"
423	
424	  buc_step "Tagging individual platform images..."
425	  for z_needed_image in ${RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS}; do
426	    local z_platform_tag="${ZRBV_VMIMAGE_TAG_PREFIX}${z_new_identity}-${z_needed_image}"
427	    local z_local_tag="localhost/podvm-${z_needed_image}:${z_new_identity}"
428	
429	    buc_step "Tagging ${z_needed_image} as ${z_platform_tag}"
430	    podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --             \
431	        "podman push ${z_local_tag} docker://${z_platform_tag}"         \
432	      || buc_die "Failed to push platform tag for ${z_needed_image}"
433	  done
434	
435	  buc_step "Update your RBRM configuration:"
436	  buc_code ""
437	  buc_code "# Add to rbmm_moorings/rbrm.env:"
438	  buc_code "RBRM_CHOSEN_IDENTITY=${z_new_identity}  # ${RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS}"
439	  buc_code ""
440	
441	  buc_success "Platform tags created for: ${RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS}"
442	}
443	
444	# Fetch VM image from GHCR container
445	rbv_fetch() {
446	  # Handle documentation mode
447	  buc_doc_brief "Fetch platform-specific VM image from GHCR to local cache"
448	  buc_doc_lines "Pulls platform-specific container image by tag"
449	  buc_doc_lines "Extracts disk image to RBRS_VMIMAGE_CACHE_DIR"
450	  buc_doc_lines "Always overwrites existing cached image"
451	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
452	
453	  # Ensure module started
454	  zrbv_sentinel
455	
456	  # Perform command
457	  buc_step "Validating platform configuration..."
458	  test -n "${RBRS_VM_PLATFORM}" || buc_die "RBRS_VM_PLATFORM not set in station config"
459	
460	  buc_step "Ensuring cache directory exists..."
461	  mkdir -p "${RBRS_VMIMAGE_CACHE_DIR}" || buc_die "Failed to create cache directory"
462	
463	  buc_step "Starting ignite VM to access podman image cache..."
464	  zrbv_ignite_bootstrap false || buc_die "Failed to start ignite machine"
465	
466	  local z_platform_tag="${ZRBV_VMIMAGE_TAG_PREFIX}${RBRM_CHOSEN_IDENTITY}-${RBRS_VM_PLATFORM}"
467	
468	  buc_step "Pulling platform-specific container: ${z_platform_tag}"
469	  podman -c "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" pull "${z_platform_tag}" \
470	    || buc_die "Failed to pull platform container: ${z_platform_tag}"
471	
472	  buc_step "Creating temporary container from platform image..."
473	  local z_temp_container_id="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/platform_container_id.txt"
474	  podman -c "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" create "${z_platform_tag}" > "${z_temp_container_id}" \
475	    || buc_die "Failed to create temporary container"
476	
477	  local z_container_id
478	  z_container_id=$(<"${z_temp_container_id}")
479	
480	  buc_step "Setting up VM temporary dir..."
481	  podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --              \
482	      "rm -rf ${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR} && mkdir -p ${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR}" \
483	    || buc_die "Failed to create VM temp directory"
484	
485	  buc_step "Extracting disk image from container..."
486	  local z_vm_temp_disk="${ZRBV_VM_TEMP_DIR}/extracted_disk_image.tar"
487	  podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" --             \
488	      "podman cp ${z_container_id}:/disk-image.tar ${z_vm_temp_disk}" \
489	    || buc_die "Failed to extract disk image from container"
490	
491	  local z_cache_file="${RBRS_VMIMAGE_CACHE_DIR}/${RBRS_VM_PLATFORM}-${RBRM_CHOSEN_IDENTITY}.tar"
492	  buc_step "Copying disk image to cache directory as -> ${z_cache_file}"
493	  podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" -- \
494	      "cat ${z_vm_temp_disk}" > "${z_cache_file}"         \
495	    || buc_die "Failed to copy disk image to cache"
496	
497	  buc_step "Cleaning up temporary container..."
498	  podman -c "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" rm "${z_container_id}" \
499	    || buc_warn "Failed to remove temporary container"
500	
501	  buc_step "Removing temporary files in VM..."
502	  podman machine ssh "${RBRM_IGNITE_MACHINE_NAME}" -- \
503	      "rm -f ${z_vm_temp_disk}"                         \
504	    || buc_warn "Failed to remove temporary disk file"
505	
506	  buc_success "VM image cached: ${z_cache_file}"
507	}
508	
509	rbv_init() {
510	  # Handle documentation mode
511	  buc_doc_brief "Initialize deploy VM from cached platform-specific image"
512	  buc_doc_lines "Uses cached disk image from RBRS_VMIMAGE_CACHE_DIR"
513	  buc_doc_lines "Initializes deploy VM with rootful mode"
514	  buc_doc_lines "Writes brand file with all RBRR_CHOSEN values"
515	  buc_doc_lines "Refuses if deploy VM already exists"
516	  buc_doc_lines "Requires rbv_fetch to be run first"
517	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
518	
519	  # Ensure module started
520	  zrbv_sentinel
521	
522	  # Perform command
523	  buc_step "Checking if deploy VM exists..."
524	  local z_machine_list
525	  z_machine_list=$(podman machine list) || buc_die "Failed to list podman machines"
526	  if [[ "${z_machine_list}" =~ ${RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME} ]]; then
527	    buc_die "Deploy VM already exists. Remove it first with rbv_nuke or manually"
528	  fi
529	
530	  buc_step "Validating platform configuration..."
531	  test -n "${RBRS_VM_PLATFORM}" || buc_die "RBRS_VM_PLATFORM not set in station config"
532	  case "${RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS}" in *"${RBRS_VM_PLATFORM}"*) ;; *)
533	    buc_die "Platform ${RBRS_VM_PLATFORM} not in manifest platforms: ${RBRM_MANIFEST_PLATFORMS}" ;; esac
534	
535	  local z_cache_file="${RBRS_VMIMAGE_CACHE_DIR}/${RBRS_VM_PLATFORM}-${RBRM_CHOSEN_IDENTITY}.tar"
536	
537	  buc_step "Checking for cached VM image..."
538	  test -f "${z_cache_file}" \
539	    || buc_die "VM image not found in cache: ${z_cache_file}" \
540	               "Run 'rbv_fetch' first to download the VM image"
541	
542	  buc_step "Initializing machine from cached image..."
543	  podman machine init --rootful --image "${z_cache_file}" "${RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME}" \
544	                                          2> "${ZRBV_DEPLOY_INIT_STDERR}"             \
545	       | "${ZRBV_SCRIPT_DIR}/rbupmis_Scrub.sh" "${ZRBV_DEPLOY_INIT_STDOUT}"             \
546	    || buc_die "Failed to initialize VM"
547	
548	  buc_step "Starting VM to write brand file..."
549	  podman machine start "${RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME}" || buc_die "Failed to start deploy VM"
550	
551	  buc_step "Generating brand file content..."
552	  local z_brand_file="${RBV_TEMP_DIR}/brand.txt"
553	  zrbv_generate_brand_file "${z_brand_file}" \
554	    || buc_die "Failed to generate brand file"
555	
556	  buc_step "Writing brand file to VM: ${ZRBV_EMPLACED_BRAND_FILE}"
557	  podman machine ssh "${RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME}" \
558	      "sudo tee ${ZRBV_EMPLACED_BRAND_FILE}" < "${z_brand_file}" > /dev/null \
559	    || buc_die "Failed to write brand file to VM"
560	
561	  buc_step "Stopping VM..."
562	  podman machine stop "${RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME}" \
563	    || buc_die "Failed to stop deploy VM"
564	
565	  buc_success "Deploy VM initialized with brand file"
566	}
567	
568	rbv_start() {
569	  # Handle documentation mode
570	  buc_doc_brief "Start Deploy VM"
571	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
572	
573	  # Ensure module started
574	  zrbv_sentinel
575	
576	  # Perform command
577	  buc_step "Starting Deploy VM..."
578	  podman machine start "${RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME}"
579	
580	  buc_success "Deploy VM started"
581	}
582	
583	rbv_stop() {
584	  # Handle documentation mode
585	  buc_doc_brief "Stop Deploy VM"
586	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
587	
588	  # Ensure module started
589	  zrbv_sentinel
590	
591	  # Perform command
592	  buc_step "Stopping deploy VM..."
593	  podman machine stop "${RBRM_DEPLOY_MACHINE_NAME}"
594	
595	  buc_success "Deploy VM stopped"
596	}
597	
598	# eof
599	
600
